metadata.paper_title: Investigating the impact of usability on software architecture through scenarios: A case study on Web systems
metadata.venue: The Journal of Systems and Software

system_context.system_description: A Web-based meeting management system aimed at the organizers of meetings where the numbers of participants and their geographic distribution make scheduling difficult.
system_context.business_goals: Allow meeting coordinators to send availability requests to a set of individuals and graphically represent availability periods.

evaluation_setup.evaluation_goal: To investigate the link between usability requirements and software architecture and to determine how usability requirements can be supported.

architectures.name: Team A's architecture
architectures.description: Business logic is well encapsulated in the Java classes and the JSP files were used only to embed some HTML code for the UI.

key_components.responsibilities: Encapsulates business logic; interacts with database.
Embed HTML code for the UI.

assumptions: Business logic represents 76% of the overall structure.

architectures.name: Team B's architecture
architectures.description: Some business logic is incorporated in the JSP component rather than in the Java component.

key_components.responsibilities: Contains presentation and some business logic; request processing; creation of objects; deciding next page.

assumptions: Business logic represents only 26% of the overall structure.

scenarios.scenario_text: Checking for correctness: Users may make errors they do not notice. Depending on the context, error correction should be enforced directly or suggested through system prompts.

Retrieving forgotten passwords: Users may forget their passwords, and retrieving them may be difficult or may cause lapses in security. Systems should provide alternative strategies to grant the user access.

Modifying interfaces: Poorly designed interfaces increase user errors. Looking at the system from a user's point of view reveals that several important aesthetic issues that need to be considered to make the site more usable, are not addressed.
Providing good help: If it is rich in functionality, then the user will inevitably need some kind of assistance.

Supporting international use: Users may want to configure an application to communicate in their own language or according to their cultural norms. Systems should be easily configurable for deployment in multiple languages.

scenario_classification.rationale: Requires the addition of a validation bean and modification of JSP files.
Requires adding a new JSP page and modifying the Java component.
Requires modification of the database and redesign of all JSP pages.
Requires modification of the database, Java queries, and translation of all JSP files.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Create a validation bean; add validation rules to User class; redesign JSP files to initialize the bean and direct request to Java classes.
Create validation bean; move embedded Java code from JSP to Java component; redesign JSP to contain only HTML.
issues_identified: Business logic was improperly incorporated in JSP; significant development effort.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Add Java code to User class to select password; create LostPassword.JSP linked to Login.JSP.
Add Java code to User class; create LostPassword.JSP.
Reworking JSP files suffering from usability issues.
Reworking JSP files suffering from usability issues.
Create new table in database for help files; add Help.JSP; add hyperlink to every Web page.
issues_identified: Dependent on the number of JSP files (15 files).

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Create new table in database; add Help.JSP; add hyperlink to every Web page.
issues_identified: Dependent on the number of JSP files (24 files).

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Duplicate database tables; rewrite queries in Java; translate HTML in JSP files.
issues_identified: Major impact; requires modification of 5 tables.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Duplicate database tables; rewrite queries in Java; translate HTML in JSP files.
issues_identified: Major impact; requires modification of 11 tables.

architectural_strengths: Team A's system was properly implemented with business logic well encapsulated in Java classes.
architectural_weaknesses: Team B's system has more JSP files than Java classes, with business logic incorporated in the JSP component.
System architecture was not designed to cope with the incorporation of late usability changes.

architecture_comparison.rationale: Better encapsulation of business logic; fewer JSP files and Database tables to modify for internationalization and help scenarios.
Poor separation of concerns; higher number of JSP files and Database tables increases effort for usability changes.

recommendations: Usability issues should be dealt with proactively during the design of the architecture.
Include architecturally sensitive usability scenarios in an architecture-centric software development process.

inference_notes: Classified all scenarios as Indirect because the paper describes them as 'changes' or 'modifications' to the existing architecture.
